chunks.title: Course Information & Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Section 600 (54988)
Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques
Spring 2026 - College Station

Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MW
Start Time: 4:10PM
End Time: 5:25PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location: HRBB 124
Credit Hours: 3

Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques. (3-0). Credit 3. Focus on teaching Natural Language Processing (NLP) fundamentals including language models, automatic syntactic processing and semantic understanding; introduction to major NLP applications including information extraction, machine translation, text summarization, dialogue systems and sentiment analysis.

Special Course Designation
None

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

* Identify and explain the key concepts and models the fundamental concepts and models for natural language processing.
* Be aware of recent trends in natural language processing research.
* Apply established techniques and propose practical methods and solutions to tackle challenges in natural language processing.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Speech and Language Processing. An Introduction to Natural Language Processing, Computational Linguistics, and Speech Recognition
ISBN: 9791221476842
Authors: Daniel Jurafsky, James H. Martin
Publication Date: 2023-01-01

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
* Assignments 31%
* Assignment 0 1%
* Assignment 1 10%
* Assignment 2 10%
* Assignment 3 10%
* Quizzes 40%
* Quiz 1 10%
* Quiz 2 10%
* Quiz 3 10%
* Quiz 4 10%
* Course Project 26%
* Project Proposal 3%
* Project Presentation 10%
* Project Report 13%
* Participation 3%
* The grading scale will be:
* A = 90-100
* B = 80-89
* C = 70-79
* D = 60-69
* F = <60

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy
* Assignment 1-3, Project Proposal, Project Report
* 1 day late: 10% penalty
* 2 days late: 20% penalty
* 3 days late: 30% penalty
* 4 days late: 50% penalty
* 5 or more days late: 100% penalty
* Assignment 0, Project Presentation
* No late submissions allowed

chunks.title: Course Schedule and Important Dates
chunks.content:
Schedule
| Week | Topics |
|---|---|
| 1 | Overview and Machine Learning Basics |
| 2 | Word Representations |
| 3 | Language Modeling, Convolutional Neural Network, Recurrent Neural Network |
| 4 | Sequential Labeling, Sequence-to-Sequence, Attention |
| 5 | Transformers |
| 6 | Contextualized Representations, Pre-Training, Large Language Models |
| 7 | Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning, Model Distillation, Evaluation |
| 8 | Instruction Tuning, Alignment, Post-Training |
| 9 | Large Reasoning Models, Test-Time Scaling, Decoding |
| 10 | Text Similarity, Retrieval-Augmented Generation, Agents |
| 11 | Multilingual Language Models, Vision-Language Models |
| 12 | Adversarial Attack and Defense, AI-Generated Text Detection |
| 13 | Non-Autoregressive Generation |
| 14 | Bias Mitigation, Hallucinations |
| 15 | Project Presentation |

Important Dates
Assignments
* Assignment 0: 1/29
* Assignment 1: 2/10
* Assignment 2: 3/3
* Assignment 3: 3/24
Quizzes
* Quiz 1: 2/4
* Quiz 2: 2/25
* Quiz 3: 3/18
* Quiz 4: 4/13
Course Project
* Project Proposal: 3/6
* Project Presentation: 4/20, 4/22, 4/27
* Project Report: 4/30

chunks.title: University Policies
chunks.content: This section outlines the university-level policies that must be included in each course syllabus. The TAMU Faculty Advisory Council established the wording of these policies.

chunks.title: CSCE 638 | Natural Language Processing: Foundations and Techniques | Spring 2026
chunks.content:
Topics: Natural Language Processing (NLP) fundamentals, language models, automatic syntactic processing, semantic understanding, information extraction, machine translation, text summarization, dialogue systems, sentiment analysis, machine learning basics, word representations, convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, sequential labeling, sequence-to-sequence, attention mechanisms, transformers, contextualized representations, pre-training, large language models, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, model distillation, evaluation, instruction tuning, alignment, post-training, large reasoning models, test-time scaling, decoding strategies, text similarity, retrieval-augmented generation, intelligent agents, multilingual language models, vision-language models, adversarial attack and defense, AI-generated text detection, non-autoregressive generation, bias mitigation, hallucinations.
Methods: Syntactic processing, semantic understanding, information extraction, machine translation, text summarization, dialogue systems, sentiment analysis, language modeling, sequential labeling, sequence-to-sequence modeling, attention mechanisms, transformer architectures, contextualized representation learning, pre-training, fine-tuning, model distillation, evaluation, instruction tuning, alignment, post-training, test-time scaling, decoding, retrieval-augmented generation, adversarial attack and defense, bias mitigation.
Niche: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning, model distillation, instruction tuning, alignment, post-training, large reasoning models, test-time scaling, decoding, retrieval-augmented generation, multilingual language models, vision-language models, adversarial attack and defense, AI-generated text detection, non-autoregressive generation, bias mitigation, hallucinations.
